learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sorting, Priority Queue, Intervals, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine managing conference rooms in an office. Whenever two meetings overlap, they require separate rooms. Your goal is to determine the minimum number of rooms needed so every meeting can be scheduled.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine managing conference rooms in an office. Whenever two meetings overlap, they require separate rooms. Your goal is to determine the minimum number of rooms needed so every meeting can be scheduled.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Sorting + Min Heap pattern whenever you need to find the minimum number of resources required for overlapping intervals.
learning.signals: Whenever the problem asks for the minimum number of resources needed for overlapping intervals, think of Sorting + Min Heap.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an array of meeting time intervals intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], return the minimum number of conference rooms required to hold all meetings.

examples.explanation: Meeting [0,30] overlaps with both other meetings, so at least two rooms are required.
The meetings do not overlap, so one room is sufficient.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Compare each meeting with every other meeting to determine the maximum number of simultaneous meetings.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Sort meetings by their start times. Keep track of currently occupied meeting rooms using a Min Heap storing meeting end times. Whenever the earliest meeting finishes before the next meeting starts, reuse that room.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: The earliest finishing meeting should always be reused first.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever the problem asks for the minimum number of resources needed for overlapping intervals, think of Sorting + Min Heap.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: The Min Heap always stores currently active meetings. Whenever a meeting ends, its room becomes available immediately. The maximum heap size represents the maximum simultaneous meetings.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever the problem asks for the minimum number of resources needed for overlapping intervals, think of Sorting + Min Heap.
commonWrongPaths: Using a Max Heap instead of a Min Heap.
Returning heap size before processing all meetings.
Confusing Meeting Rooms I with Meeting Rooms II.

hints: Store ending times in a Min Heap.
commonMisconceptions: Using a Max Heap instead of a Min Heap.
Returning heap size before processing all meetings.
Confusing Meeting Rooms I with Meeting Rooms II.

pattern.name: Sorting + Min Heap
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever the problem asks for the minimum number of resources needed for overlapping intervals, think of Sorting + Min Heap.

approaches.title: Compare Every Meeting
approaches.idea: Compare each meeting with every other meeting to determine the maximum number of simultaneous meetings.
approaches.dryRun: Check overlap with every other meeting.

code.java: Nested loop solution omitted due to quadratic complexity.

approaches.title: Sorting + Two Pointers
approaches.idea: Separate all start and end times into two arrays, sort them independently, and use two pointers to count active meetings.
approaches.dryRun: Starts increase rooms, ends free rooms.

approaches.title: Sorting + Min Heap
approaches.idea: Sort meetings by start time. Use a Min Heap storing meeting end times. If the earliest ending meeting finishes before the current meeting starts, remove it from the heap because that room becomes available. Insert the current meeting's end time. The maximum heap size equals the minimum number of meeting rooms required.
approaches.intuition: The Min Heap always stores currently active meetings. Whenever a meeting ends, its room becomes available immediately. The maximum heap size represents the maximum simultaneous meetings.
algorithm: Insert the end time of the first meeting.
If heap top <= current start, remove the earliest ending meeting.
Insert the current meeting's end time.

code.javascript: JavaScript requires implementing a Min Heap manually or using a Priority Queue library. The algorithm remains identical: sort meetings, reuse rooms when the earliest meeting ends, otherwise allocate a new room.

steps: Maximum heap size is the answer.

revisionNotes: Maximum heap size is the answer.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved using two pointers?
Why is a Min Heap required?
How would you assign room numbers?
Can you return the meeting schedule?
What if meetings can be rescheduled?
interviewTips: Describe how the Min Heap represents occupied rooms.
State O(n log n) time and O(n) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: The earliest finishing meeting should always be reused first.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Sort → Min Heap → Reuse Room → Heap Size.

importantPoints: Min Heap stores end times only.
commonMistakes: Using a Max Heap instead of a Min Heap.
Returning heap size before processing all meetings.
Confusing Meeting Rooms I with Meeting Rooms II.
